title: Lagging Theory for Periodic Hydraulic Head Signals in Aquifers

research.question: Can separate flux-adjustment and inter-domain head-equilibration times reconcile hydraulic diffusivities inferred from periodic-head amplitude damping and phase lag?
research.system: One-dimensional periodic head propagation from a river, coast, or surface boundary into a heterogeneous aquifer represented by a causal Darcy-law memory model.
research.inputData: Multi-frequency river-stage and groundwater-head records at multiple distances from Tuolumne Meadows, USA, and the Meghna River, Bangladesh.
research.method: Replace instantaneous Darcy response with a two-lag causal kernel, derive harmonic amplitude and phase, quantify the phase-amplitude mismatch, extract coherent transfer functions, fit lagging and classical models, and evaluate sensitivity, AIC, and bootstrap uncertainty.
research.benchmark: The homogeneous diffusion solution, numerical frequency-domain verification, independent site lithology and hydraulic-property ranges, residual RMSE, and AIC.
research.uncertainty: Delete-one-block jackknife errors weight the inversion. Five thousand Gaussian amplitude-phase realizations are refitted, interquartile-filtered, and summarized by ensemble means and one-sigma intervals; a separate 1000-resample constituent bootstrap produces diffusivity densities. Joint estimation of diffusivity and both lags requires multiple well-separated frequencies and at least two distances, while a one-frequency fit fixes one lag a priori.
research.conclusion: Two field-scale delays jointly fit amplitude and phase with more plausible diffusivities at both sites, while reducing to classical diffusion when both delays vanish.

narrative.throughline: A periodic groundwater wave carries two independent signatures because discharge pathways adjust on one clock and communicating storage domains equilibrate on another.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience knows that a periodic river or tide signal becomes smaller and later farther into an aquifer.
narrative.stakes: Forcing both signatures into one classical diffusivity can produce mutually inconsistent or geologically implausible aquifer properties.
narrative.resolution: The two clocks return to the field transects as jointly fitted amplitude envelopes and phase shifts, bounded as upscaled effective parameters rather than literal delays.

background.premise: Periodic boundary signals act as passive tracers of aquifer hydraulic properties.
background.visibleEvidence: A sinusoidal river stage enters an aquifer and becomes a damped delayed groundwater wave along a transect.
background.whyNow: This introduces the measured scientific object before any constitutive law.
background.boundary: The method assumes a robust dominant harmonic and a constrained boundary signal.
background.sourceExcerpt: 1990; LeRoux Such analytical solutions are widely applied as passive tracers for
background.visualObject: Boundary sinusoid propagating through observation wells
background.motionPurpose: Make amplitude loss and phase delay visible as separate geometric changes.
background.stepDetail: Drive the boundary sinusoid, copy it inland, shrink its envelope, and shift its crest at each well.
background.transitionBridge: The two observable changes produce two classical diffusivity estimates.

background.premise: Classical homogeneous diffusion often yields different diffusivities from amplitude and phase observations.
background.visibleEvidence: Amplitude-derived and phase-derived diffusivity markers split apart despite coming from the same wave.
background.whyNow: This is the paper's central measurement puzzle.
background.boundary: The mismatch is attributed to conceptual-model simplification and data limitations rather than a failure of diffusion physics itself.
background.sourceExcerpt: estimates derived from the phase shift rarely match those inferred from amplitude
background.visualObject: One groundwater wave feeding two separated diffusivity gauges
background.motionPurpose: Expose the internal contradiction of a single-parameter inversion.
background.stepDetail: Measure damping and lag from the same wells, invert each with the classical formula, and separate the resulting D markers.
background.transitionBridge: The split calls for two field-scale response clocks.

background.premise: The proposed lags are causal field-scale memory parameters rather than literal time shifts.
background.visibleEvidence: A memory kernel weights past gradients while two clocks alter attenuation and phase frequency-dependently.
background.whyNow: This prevents a misleading animation of the model as simply sliding a signal in time.
background.boundary: Specific heterogeneity mechanisms are not uniquely identified from the fitted lags.
background.sourceExcerpt: attenuation and phase in a frequency-dependent way rather than shifting the signal
background.visualObject: Decaying memory kernel with flux and head relaxation clocks
background.motionPurpose: Replace a literal horizontal shift with weighted history and frequency-dependent deformation.
background.stepDetail: Stack past gradients behind the present, fade them by kernel weight, and let each clock reshape envelope or phase response.
background.transitionBridge: The memory object becomes the modified Darcy constitutive relation.

methods.input: The history of hydraulic gradients at a field-scale point
methods.output: A causal Darcy flux defined by a nonnegative kernel
methods.validity: The kernel uses only past gradients and integrates to effective hydraulic conductivity.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1, Equations (2)-(3)
methods.sourceExcerpt: and heat transfer, we therefore represent Darcy’s law at the field scale
methods.visualObject: Past-gradient traces fading into a present flux arrow
methods.motionPurpose: Show that present discharge is assembled from weighted gradient history.
methods.stepDetail: Order gradient snapshots by look-back time, weight them with the kernel, integrate their contributions, and form the present flux.
methods.transitionBridge: A two-parameter kernel compresses the memory history into two lags.

methods.input: The causal memory relation and mass conservation
methods.output: A Jeffreys-type head equation containing tau_q and tau_h
methods.validity: Continuity is enforced at present time and delay enters only through the constitutive relation; zero lags recover classical diffusion.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Sections 2.1-2.2, Equations (4)-(7)
methods.sourceExcerpt: gives the classic diffusion equation Eq. (7) reduces to classical diffusion when
methods.visualObject: Flux and head clocks entering opposite sides of the diffusion balance
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal how each lag modifies a distinct derivative while conserving mass.
methods.stepDetail: Insert the two-lag discharge law into continuity, replace transmissivity over storativity by D, and group the two time operators.
methods.transitionBridge: Periodic boundary forcing turns the PDE into a complex wavenumber.

methods.input: The lagging PDE with sinusoidal boundary head and far-field mean head
methods.output: Closed-form head amplitude A_h and phase phi from complex wavenumber kappa
methods.validity: The branch with negative real part is chosen so the wave attenuates inland.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.3, Equations (10)-(15)
methods.sourceExcerpt: at Assuming harmonic solutions gives ℎD ∝ ������������������D ������������������D������D with the dispersion
methods.visualObject: Complex wavenumber splitting into damping alpha and phase gradient beta
methods.motionPurpose: Turn one propagating wave into separately inspectable envelope and crest motion.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the harmonic ansatz, choose the decaying root, separate real and imaginary parts, and label amplitude and phase.
methods.transitionBridge: Alpha and beta generate the two apparent classical diffusivities.

methods.input: Lagging-model damping alpha and phase gradient beta
methods.output: R = D_phase/D_amplitude = alpha^2/beta^2
methods.validity: Both apparent diffusivities use the standard classical inversions applied to the lagging response.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.4, Equations (20)-(21)
methods.sourceExcerpt: spectral conventions (DFT, autospectrum/cross- inconsistent diffusivity estimates from phase and amplitude data.
methods.visualObject: Mismatch map centered on an R = 1 contour
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the split diffusivity gauges into a signed regime map.
methods.stepDetail: Square alpha and beta, form their ratio, center the color scale at one, and locate amplitude- or phase-dominant sectors.
methods.transitionBridge: The mismatch map is next tested for sensitivity to lag ratio and dimensionless frequency.

methods.input: Lag ratio and dimensionless frequency values along a Morris trajectory
methods.output: One elementary effect for each input factor trajectory and model output
methods.validity: The Morris finite difference uses the stated normalized grid step for amplitude or phase output.

methods.sourceExcerpt: is quantified using the Morris elementary-effects (EE) method
methods.visualObject: Morris trajectory stepping once along each input axis
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the output change caused by one factor step at a time.
methods.stepDetail: Advance one normalized factor along a trajectory and display the resulting elementary effect.

methods.input: Elementary effects collected across all Morris trajectories
methods.output: Absolute mean elementary effect mu-star for each factor
methods.validity: The absolute mean summarizes effect magnitude across all optimized trajectories.

methods.sourceExcerpt: trajectories is summarized by the absolute mean
methods.visualObject: Signed elementary-effect marks folding into one absolute-mean bar
methods.motionPurpose: Summarize factor influence without cancellation between positive and negative effects.
methods.stepDetail: Take each effect magnitude and average the magnitudes across trajectories.
methods.transitionBridge: Variation around the mean reveals interaction or nonlinearity.

methods.input: Elementary effects and their trajectory mean for each factor
methods.output: Standard deviation sigma for each factor's elementary effects
methods.validity: The reported deviation measures interactions or nonlinear response in the Morris design.

methods.visualObject: Elementary-effect marks spreading around their mean
methods.motionPurpose: Make cross-trajectory variability visible as a second sensitivity dimension.
methods.stepDetail: Measure the spread of each factor's effects around its displayed mean.
methods.transitionBridge: Magnitude and spread combine into one overall ranking index.

methods.input: Absolute mean mu-star and standard deviation sigma for each factor
methods.output: Overall Morris sensitivity index delta for each factor
methods.validity: Equation 25 combines the two metrics into the single factor-ranking index used in the study.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.5 Global sensitivity analysis, Equation 25
methods.sourceExcerpt: the two metrics were combined
methods.visualObject: Orthogonal magnitude and spread bars closing into one radial index
methods.motionPurpose: Reduce the two Morris summaries to the overall effect used for factor comparison.
methods.stepDetail: Join the absolute-mean and deviation components into one overall sensitivity length.
methods.transitionBridge: Field records are prepared before their measured harmonics enter inversion.

symbols.meaning: Complex spatial wavenumber with damping coefficient and phase gradient
symbols.transition: Split the wave into envelope alpha and crest-motion beta.
Transform the shrinking wave envelope into Ah.
Combine the two diffusivity gauges into R.

return.visualObject: Tuolumne and Meghna boundary-to-well transects with jointly fitted envelopes phase crests two clocks and bootstrap D densities
return.claim: Separating flux adjustment from inter-domain equilibration reconciles the observed phase-amplitude mismatch and yields site-plausible diffusivities for the two analyzed aquifers.
return.claimBoundary: The lags are effective upscaled memory parameters rather than unique mechanism measurements. Joint estimation of diffusivity and both lags requires multiple well-separated frequencies and at least two distances; with only one frequency, one lag parameter is fixed a priori and the remaining two are estimated. Process-based links to measurable site properties remain future work.

return.minimalText: Two clocks reconcile one groundwater wave

visual.primaryObject: Periodic boundary head wave propagating along an aquifer transect
visual.accentObject: Flux-lag and head-lag clocks on a phase-amplitude mismatch map
visual.measuredQuantity: Head amplitude ratio and phase lag by frequency and distance
visual.geometryNotes: Preserve a continuous boundary-to-well sinusoid. Damping must shrink the envelope and phase must shift crests independently; never animate either lag as a literal rigid time translation. Use density curves and confidence bands for uncertainty.
